mnemonics: Countably Infinite Sets are like Natural Numbers (ℵ₀), and their arithmetic behaves like the 'smallest infinity'. For example, ℵ₀ + ℵ₀ = ℵ₀, just like adding two infinite hotels (Hilbert's Hotel) still gives one infinite hotel.
For exponentiation, remember '2 to the power of a cardinal' defines the power set. So, |P(A)| = 2^{|A|}. This is your bridge between set operations and cardinal arithmetic.
keyPoints: The cardinality of the set of natural numbers ℕ is denoted by ℵ₀ (aleph-null), which is the smallest infinite cardinal.
For any infinite set A, |A × A| = |A|. This is a crucial property used in proofs for addition and multiplication.
Cantor's Theorem: For any set A, its power set P(A) has a strictly greater cardinality, |P(A)| = 2^{|A|} > |A|.
commonMistakes: Assuming the arithmetic of infinite cardinals follows the same rules as finite numbers. For example, ℵ₀ + 1 = ℵ₀, not ℵ₀ + 1.
Incorrectly applying the power set operation. The cardinality of P(A) is 2^{|A|}, not |A|². This is a fundamental distinction between exponentiation and multiplication.
